one_line_takeaway: GREW ports LLM-style green-red logit-bias watermarking to sequential recommenders via semantic-aware hashing, decision-boundary-confined injection, and confidence-adaptive scaling, achieving black-box statistical ownership verification (Z-test, threshold Z>4) that survives DFME model-extraction attacks (V@1 up to 100%) while a memorization-based baseline (AOW) collapses to 0% detection under the same attack.
key_finding: On watermark validity, GREW reaches 100% detection confidence (P<1e-4) across all three datasets and all three base models, matching AOW's clean-model detection rate. Under DFME model extraction, AOW's verification (V~@1) collapses from 100.00% to 0.00% in most settings (e.g., SAS and NARM on all three datasets), while GREW retains V~@1 = 95.90%-100.00% across the same conditions -- notably even when GREW was tested under a harder heterogeneous extraction setting (NARM teacher -> BERT student) versus AOW's easier homogeneous setting (BERT->BERT). On stealthiness (Table 3, ML-1M), AOW at its practical n=5 trigger length is 100.0% detectable by a simple popularity/transition anomaly detector versus 0.00% detectability for GREW, and AOW's watermark retention in extraction data is 0% versus 100.00% for GREW. On utility preservation, GREW's Recall@10 stays closer to the unwatermarked base model than AOW's in the reported cases (e.g., NARM/ML-1M: Base 27.56%, GREW 26.29%, AOW 23.15%). Table 2 shows unwatermarked base models have a natural HR@20 (green-item hit rate) of ~29.9%-33.9% versus a target density gamma=0.33, while GREW watermarked models shift HR@20 to 44.96%-58.73% with corresponding Z@20 scores of 86.02-182.02 (all P<1e-6). Table 4 shows GREW also degrades extraction fidelity itself: SASRec Agr@10 (extracted-vs-victim Top-10 overlap) drops from 0.424 (unwatermarked) to 0.326 (GREW-watermarked) under DFME, while the Z-score on the extracted student model remains 5.056 (BERT) / 6.470 (SAS), both above the Z>4 ownership threshold. Ablations (Table 5, ML-1M/BERT4Rec) show removing Semantic-Consistent Hashing drops Z@20 from 87.39 to 68.93; removing alpha_local drops R@10 to 19.33% (largest utility loss among variants); removing alpha_global drops Z@20 to 85.00 with degraded utility/detection balance. Hyperparameter analysis: k_cand=30 causes utility collapse (R@10 to 9.72%) with failed watermark injection; k_cand=50 is identified as optimal (Z@20=74.69) balancing utility and verifiability; delta=2 gives R@10=20.25% with Z=66.52 as the most balanced injection magnitude.
core_contribution: GREW (Green-REd Watermarking) is a black-box watermarking framework for sequential recommender systems that adapts the LLM green-red logit-bias watermarking paradigm to recommendation ranking. Instead of forcing a model to memorize predefined out-of-distribution trigger interaction sequences (the prior 'memorization' paradigm, e.g. AOW), GREW uses a secret key to partition the item space into 'green' items (soft-promoted) and 'red' items (anchors) at each recommendation step, and biases ranking logits toward green items just enough to be statistically detectable while preserving ranking fidelity. Three modules make this recommendation-specific: (1) Semantic-Consistent Hashing projects item embeddings to a 1-D coordinate via a secret-key-randomized projection vector and Random-Fourier-Feature sinusoidal hashing, so that green-item assignment clusters semantically similar items rather than scattering by ID, keeping promoted items aligned with plausible user interests; (2) Decision-Aligned Masking restricts injection to a competitive item subset near the Top-K decision boundary (score >= the k_cand-th ranked item's score, e.g. k_cand=100), preventing irrelevant tail items from being artificially promoted; (3) Confidence-Adaptive Scaling multiplies the injection magnitude delta by a local factor (Shannon entropy of the Top-K softmax, normalized by log K) so perturbation is stronger when the model is uncertain and weaker when confident, and a global factor alpha_global that is adjusted via a feedback rule (alpha_global += eta*(tau - r_bar)) to hold the aggregate green-item hit rate near a target. Ownership verification is a one-proportion Z-test comparing the observed green-item hit rate in a suspect model's black-box Top-K outputs against the expected background rate gamma under the secret-key-reproduced partition, using Z = (p_hat - gamma) / sqrt(gamma(1-gamma)/N) with an ownership-claim threshold Z>4 (P<1e-5).
threat_model: Defender is the owner of a proprietary sequential recommender model deployed via a black-box API (returns only Top-K item lists per query). Adversary can query the API and attempts (a) unauthorized commercial reuse/redeployment of the model, and (b) model extraction attacks (the paper specifically evaluates DFME, a data-free model extraction attack) to train a surrogate/student model that replicates the victim's ranking behavior without direct model access. The owner's goal is to embed a signal detectable purely from aggregated black-box Top-K outputs (no internal access needed for verification) that (i) survives extraction/distillation into a stolen surrogate and (ii) does not degrade recommendation quality or create statistically detectable anomalies (e.g., via an anomaly detector based on item popularity/transition patterns) that an adversary could use to locate and strip the watermark before or during extraction.
limitations: The paper's own text does not report statistical significance testing (e.g., confidence intervals, multiple seeds) for the utility metrics in Table 1, so small Recall/NDCG deltas between GREW and AOW are not accompanied by variance estimates. Robustness is evaluated against only one extraction attack family (DFME); the paper does not test other extraction or watermark-removal strategies (e.g., fine-tuning, pruning, adaptive adversaries aware of the green-red scheme). The extraction comparison between GREW and AOW is explicitly asymmetric (GREW tested under a harder heterogeneous NARM->BERT setting, AOW under an easier homogeneous BERT->BERT setting), which the authors frame as disadvantaging GREW, but this also means the two are not evaluated under identical extraction conditions elsewhere in the same table. The stealthiness/anomaly-detector evaluation (Table 3) is confined to the ML-1M dataset and does not report results on Amazon Beauty or Steam Review. No adaptive adversary is considered that specifically knows the green-red hashing mechanism and attempts to reverse-engineer or filter it (the anomaly detector used is a generic popularity/transition-pattern detector, not one tailored to green-red schemes). The paper is authored by academic researchers (Chongqing University, University of Leicester) with code released, but no third-party or industrial deployment validation is reported.

quantitative_results: GREW watermark detection confidence: 100% (P<1e-4) across all 3 datasets x 3 base models (Table 1)
AOW verification on extracted (student) model collapses from 100.00% to 0.00% in most cases (V~@1, Table 1)
GREW verification on extracted model retains 95.90%-100.00% V~@1 across datasets/models (Table 1)
HR@20 shift: Base models ~29.91%-33.93%, GREW watermarked 44.96%-58.73%; Z@20: BERT 114.21, SAS 182.02, NARM 86.02, all P<1e-6 (Table 2)
Stealthiness (ML-1M): AOW (n=5) Injection 10.0%, Detectability 100.0%, extraction Retention 0%; GREW Injection 0%, Detectability 0.00%, extraction Retention 100.00% (Table 3)
AOW n=20 variant: extraction V@1 drops to 0.00%, V~@5 6.51%, V~@10 11.21%, V~@20 26.13% (Table 3)
GREW under heterogeneous NARM->BERT extraction: V@1 100.0%, V~@1 95.90%, V~@5 99.65%, V~@10 99.94%, V~@20 100.00% (Table 3)
Post-DFME-extraction Agreement@10: BERT 0.498->0.419, SAS 0.424->0.326 (unwatermarked base+DFME vs GREW+DFME) (Table 4)
Post-DFME Z@20 on extracted student model: BERT 5.056, SAS 6.470, both exceeding the Z>4 ownership threshold (Table 4)
Hyperparameter sweep: k_cand=30 causes R@10 collapse to 9.72%; k_cand=50 optimal at Z@20=74.69; delta=2 gives R@10=20.25% with Z=66.52 (Section 5.5)
Ablation (ML-1M/BERT4Rec): full GREW Z@20=87.39; w/o Semantic hashing Z@20=68.93; w/o alpha_local R@10=19.33% (lowest utility); w/o alpha_global Z@20=85.00 (Table 5)
baselines_compared: AOW (memorization-based recommender watermarking baseline, Dang et al. non-influential watermarking family cited as [34])
